<style>
</style>

**ANNEX
A — USE CASE CATALOGUE**

**Candidate Use Cases for the**

**Agentic Workforce Platform**

**Document Type:** Business Requirements Document —
Annex

**Companion to:** Agentic
Workforce Platform BRD v0.2

**Version:** 0.1 — Initial
Draft

**Status:** Draft for Review

**Date:** April 2026

**Classification:** Internal
— Confidential

# A.1 Purpose of this Annex

This Annex
presents a catalogue of candidate use cases for the Agentic Workforce Platform
across Investec's businesses and control functions. Its purpose is to
illustrate the breadth of opportunity, to support prioritisation conversations
with business sponsors, and to anchor the Platform's delivery roadmap in
concrete outcomes.

The catalogue
is deliberately broad rather than selective — it is a demand-side view intended
to provoke discussion, not a commitment. Each use case will be taken through a
formal qualification process before being selected for build, with a documented
business case, control assessment and jurisdictional review. Inclusion in this
Annex is not itself an approval to proceed.

## A.1.1 How to Read Each Use Case

Each use case
in this Annex follows a consistent structure:

•     A concise description of the business process and the
problem the use case addresses.

•     The composition of the proposed digital worker team —
roles, agent types and the named human Accountable Supervisor role.

•     Expected benefits across four lenses: cycle time, unit
cost, risk and control posture, and capacity release.

•     Indicative readiness — an assessment of the work
required to deploy the use case safely.

•     Key risks and caveats specific to the use case.

## A.1.2 How Readiness Is Indicated

Readiness is
expressed as an indicative delivery phase and is intended to guide sequencing,
not to commit the programme. Phase 1 (0 to 12 months) use cases are those where
the process is relatively contained, the playbook is well-understood, and the
control envelope is clearly definable. Phase 2 (12 to 18 months) use cases
typically require more work on data foundations, inter-system integration or
change-management. Phase 3 (18 months plus) use cases extend the Platform into
more novel territory where additional governance maturity is advantageous.

Phasing will be
revisited during detailed design and ongoing portfolio reviews with the Design
Authority and business sponsors.

## A.1.3 How Benefits Are Estimated

Benefit
intensity in this Annex is qualitative — high, meaningful or modest — rather
than quantitative. Converting these intensities into concrete financial numbers
requires bottom-up analysis per team (baseline volumes, throughput, unit cost,
error rates, control burden). That work will be undertaken for any use case
selected for the funded roadmap. The intent here is to guide comparison and
prioritisation, not to pre-commit to outcomes.

# A.2 Catalogue Overview

The catalogue
contains eighteen use cases organised into six categories spanning front-office
operations, wealth and credit workflows, control functions and corporate
services. The figure below provides a single-page view of the full catalogue,
with indicative impact and readiness for each use case.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

*Figure A.1. Candidate use case catalogue
— 18 use cases across six categories.*

# A.3 Benefits Heatmap

Different use
cases deliver value differently. Some are primarily about freeing human
capacity for higher-value work; others are primarily about strengthening
control posture; others offer transformational cost and cycle-time
improvements. The heatmap below surfaces those differences across the full
catalogue.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

*Figure A.2. Expected benefit intensity
by dimension, per use case.*

Three patterns are worth noting:

•     Client Onboarding and Payments Operations use cases
deliver broadly-distributed benefits — they are strong candidates to anchor the
early roadmap.

•     Risk, Compliance & Audit use cases tilt towards
control-posture benefits rather than cost reduction — they strengthen the
second line of defence rather than re-shape economics.

•     Corporate Functions use cases (UC-16 to UC-18) are
steadier but less dramatic — they may be best delivered once the Platform is
stable, as scale workloads.

# A.4 Category Summary

The table below
summarises each category: the number of use cases included, the primary
business sponsor(s), the dominant Agent Team archetype, and the anticipated
regulatory and control focus.

| **Category**                 | **Cases** | **Primary Sponsor(s)**           | **Dominant Archetype**   | **Regulatory Focus**                       |
| ---------------------------- | --------- | -------------------------------- | ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------ |
| Client<br> Onboarding & KYC  | 3         | Private<br> Banking · CIB        | Maker-Checker-Supervisor | AML/CTF<br> · KYC · Consumer Duty          |
| Payments & Operations        | 3         | Banking Operations               | Case-Investigator        | Payments rules · screening                 |
| Wealth<br> & Investment      | 3         | W&I                              | Research-Report          | Suitability<br> · MiFID II · Consumer Duty |
| Credit & Lending             | 3         | CIB · Private Banking            | Research-Report          | Credit risk · regulatory capital           |
| Risk,<br> Compliance & Audit | 3         | Group<br> Risk · Compliance · IA | Monitor-Triage-Escalate  | Cross-regime<br> · internal control        |
| Corporate Functions          | 3         | HR · Procurement · Finance       | Case-Investigator        | Data protection · supplier risk            |

# A.5 Client Onboarding & KYC

Client
onboarding is a natural fit for agentic automation: the process is structured,
well-documented in jurisdictional playbooks, and the controls are mature.
Digital worker teams can execute the playbook end-to-end under human
supervision, with significant improvement in cycle time and consistency.

**UC-01**   **Private Client Onboarding**

**Audience / sponsor:** *UK Private Client · CIPC*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 1

#### Description

Execute the UK
Private Client onboarding playbook from application receipt to handoff to
servicing. The team performs identity verification, documents capture,
sanctions and PEP screening, risk factor collation, risk rating and QA.

#### Team composition

Maker-Checker-Supervisor
archetype. 1 Supervisor, 4-5 Makers (IDV, Screening, Data Capture, Rating), 1-2
QA. Named Accountable Supervisor: Head of Onboarding (UK).

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | High.<br> Target 50% reduction in end-to-end cycle time; early internal benchmarks<br> suggest 3-5 days becoming same-day for clean cases. |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Cost<br> reduction   | High. Onboarding is headcount-heavy; agentic execution<br> reduces manual effort on clean-path cases substantially.                        |
| Risk<br> & control   | High.<br> Consistent, auditable playbook execution reduces control-break risk and<br> improves evidence quality for assurance.             |
| Capacity<br> release | High. Releases specialist onboarders to focus on complex<br> and high-value cases.                                                         |

#### Key risks and caveats

Completeness of
applicant data; IDV provider rate limits; PEP/adverse-media escalations require
careful handling; Consumer Duty considerations for private clients.

**UC-02**   **Corporate Client Onboarding**

**Audience / sponsor:** *CIB · Corporate and SME segments*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 2

#### Description

Onboarding for
corporate clients — typically more complex than private clients due to
ownership structures, ultimate beneficial ownership discovery, and
multi-jurisdictional considerations. The team decomposes entity structures,
gathers documentation from registries and the client, performs screening across
the structure, and drafts the risk rating.

#### Team composition

Maker-Checker-Supervisor
with additional researchers. 1 Supervisor, 5-6 Makers (Structure, Registry,
Document, Screening, Data Capture), 1 Researcher, 2 QA. Named Accountable
Supervisor: Head of Corporate Onboarding.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | High.<br> Corporate onboarding is slower than private — agentic execution has more room<br> to compress elapsed time. |
| -------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | High. Each corporate case consumes substantial specialist<br> analyst effort.                                         |
| Risk<br> & control   | High.<br> Tax transparency, UBO requirements and sanctions exposure all benefit from<br> consistent execution.        |
| Capacity<br> release | Meaningful. Specialist analysts spend more time on<br> genuinely complex structures.                                  |

#### Key risks and caveats

Quality and
coverage of corporate registry data across jurisdictions; UBO discovery
heuristics require careful governance; sanctions exposure through complex
structures is a known risk.

**UC-03**   **Periodic KYC Refresh**

**Audience / sponsor:** *All client-facing businesses (UK, SA, CI, CH, etc.)*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 1

#### Description

Periodic
refresh of KYC records for existing clients (low/medium/high risk cycles). The
team pulls current records, compares against refreshed data sources, identifies
gaps and triggers, performs refreshed screening and either confirms no change
or raises a review case to a human reviewer.

#### Team composition

Monitor-Triage-Escalate.
1 Supervisor, 3-4 Makers (Refresh, Screening, Change Detection), 1 QA. Named
Accountable Supervisor: Head of Financial Crime Operations per jurisdiction.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | High.<br> Refresh cycles currently run long and create periodic backlogs; continuous<br> execution smooths this. |
| -------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | High. Refresh is a perennial, large-volume workload.                                                             |
| Risk<br> & control   | Meaningful.<br> Drives consistent cadence and reduces the risk of late refreshes.                                |
| Capacity<br> release | High. Frees analysts from scheduled batch work for genuine<br> review cases.                                     |

#### Key risks and caveats

Volume is high
and concentrated at cycle boundaries — scaling requires capacity planning;
data-source access and residency; retention of refreshed evidence.

# A.6 Payments & Operations

Payment
operations is characterised by high-volume, exception-driven workflows with
well-defined rulebooks. The combination of volume, repetitiveness and
structural containment makes this a strong fit for agentic execution — provided
that the control envelope is tightly defined.

**UC-04**   **Payment Exception Repair**

**Audience / sponsor:** *Banking Operations (SA and UK)*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 1

#### Description

Investigate and
repair payments flagged as exceptions by the payments engine (missing/invalid
data, format issues, routing failures). The team classifies the exception,
applies the appropriate repair playbook within strict limit matrices, and
resubmits — or escalates to a human operator when outside tolerance.

#### Team composition

Case-Investigator
archetype. 1 Supervisor, 2-4 Makers (Investigator, Repair, Triage), 1 QA. Named
Accountable Supervisor: Head of Payments Operations per region.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | High.<br> Same-day resolution for clean exceptions versus next-day today. |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | High. Exception handling is currently a major consumer of<br> Ops hours.  |
| Risk<br> & control   | Modest.<br> Tightly-scoped limit matrices keep control risk contained.    |
| Capacity<br> release | High. Ops staff redirected to genuinely complex<br> investigations.       |

#### Key risks and caveats

Integration
with payments engines is bespoke per rail; strict limit matrices per payment
type must be enforced by policy-as-code; SWIFT/SEPA/SARPS nuances vary by
jurisdiction.

**UC-05**   **Reconciliation Break Investigation**

**Audience / sponsor:** *Finance Ops · Banking Ops*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 2

#### Description

Investigate
breaks between internal ledgers, custodian statements and counterparty records.
The team retrieves matching records, applies reconciliation rules, identifies
the likely cause of the break, and proposes or applies the resolution within
defined limits; otherwise escalates.

#### Team composition

Case-Investigator.
1 Supervisor, 2-3 Investigators, 1 QA. Named Accountable Supervisor: Head of
Reconciliations.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | Meaningful.<br> Reconciliation breaks often wait until a human reviewer has time; continuous<br> execution reduces ageing. |
| -------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | Meaningful. Reconciliations teams are moderately-sized;<br> impact scales with scope.                                      |
| Risk<br> & control   | Modest.<br> Most breaks are small; consistent investigation improves evidence quality.                                     |
| Capacity<br> release | High. Reconciliations analysts redirected to genuine<br> investigations.                                                   |

#### Key risks and caveats

Data access
across custodians and counterparties is heterogeneous; resolving breaks by
applying journal entries requires strict authorisation controls.

**UC-06**   **Screening Alert Triage**

**Audience / sponsor:** *Financial Crime Operations (UK, SA, CI, etc.)*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 1

#### Description

Triage
low-severity sanctions, PEP and adverse media screening alerts. The team
dispositions clean false positives under tight policy, escalates potential true
positives for human decision, and produces a structured summary for the human
reviewer in every escalation case.

#### Team composition

Monitor-Triage-Escalate.
1 Supervisor, 3-4 Makers (Sanctions, PEP, Adverse Media triage), 1 QA. Named
Accountable Supervisor: MLRO or delegate per jurisdiction.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | High.<br> Alert backlogs at cycle boundaries collapse.                               |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Cost<br> reduction   | High. FCO teams spend large portions of time on false<br> positives.                 |
| Risk<br> & control   | Meaningful.<br> Policy-driven dispositioning produces a consistent, auditable trail. |
| Capacity<br> release | High. MLROs and analysts focus on genuine concerns.                                  |

#### Key risks and caveats

True-positive /
false-positive dispositioning must be tightly governed; regulator expectations
on screening logic; a well-designed model risk classification is essential.

# A.7 Wealth & Investment

Wealth &
Investment use cases focus on preparation, monitoring and checking — freeing
wealth advisers and portfolio managers to spend more time with clients and on
investment decisions. Suitability, Consumer Duty and conduct considerations are
particularly important for W&I teams.

**UC-07**   **Client Review Pack Preparation**

**Audience / sponsor:** *W&I Wealth Advisers and Portfolio Managers*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 2

#### Description

Prepare client
review packs ahead of periodic meetings — performance summary, attribution,
benchmark comparison, fee summary, tax lot positions, suitability-relevant life
events, and commentary prompts. The team assembles from internal sources and
produces a draft pack for adviser review and personalisation.

#### Team composition

Research-Report
archetype. 1 Supervisor, 2-3 Researchers (Performance, Positions, Life Events),
1 QA, 1 Reporter. Named Accountable Supervisor: Head of Wealth Operations.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | High.<br> Pack preparation today consumes adviser or paraplanner hours; agentic<br> execution compresses this dramatically.   |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | Meaningful. Paraplanners redirected to higher-value work;<br> fewer external production costs.                                |
| Risk<br> & control   | Modest.<br> Consistent pack format improves audit quality; Consumer Duty considerations<br> remain an adviser responsibility. |
| Capacity<br> release | High. Advisers spend more time with clients rather than<br> preparing.                                                        |

#### Key risks and caveats

Consumer Duty —
the adviser remains accountable for the quality and suitability of the client
conversation; generated commentary must be clearly distinguishable from adviser
judgement.

**UC-08**   **Portfolio Drift Monitoring**

**Audience / sponsor:** *W&I Portfolio Management · Compliance*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 1

#### Description

Continuously
monitor portfolios against mandates, investment policy statements and
house-view guidance. Detect drift, concentration exceedances and benchmark
deviations, summarise and escalate to the responsible portfolio manager with
suggested rebalancing actions.

#### Team composition

Monitor-Triage-Escalate.
1 Supervisor, 2-3 Monitors, 1 QA. Named Accountable Supervisor: Head of
Portfolio Management or equivalent.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | Meaningful.<br> Drift detection moves from periodic review to continuous.         |
| -------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | Modest. The work is cost-light today but often late.                              |
| Risk<br> & control   | Meaningful.<br> Continuous monitoring reduces conduct and suitability risk.       |
| Capacity<br> release | Meaningful. Portfolio managers act on signals rather than<br> searching for them. |

#### Key risks and caveats

Investment
guidance quality depends on data; rebalancing suggestions must never be
executed without portfolio manager approval; conduct and market-abuse
considerations.

**UC-09**   **Suitability Check Assistance**

**Audience / sponsor:** *W&I Advisory · Compliance Monitoring*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 2

#### Description

Assist
second-line monitoring of suitability by reviewing a sample of advice
interactions against the suitability framework, identifying prima-facie
indicators and drafting review memos for human suitability officers.

#### Team composition

Research-Report.
1 Supervisor, 2 Researchers, 1 QA, 1 Reporter. Named Accountable Supervisor:
Head of Advisory Compliance.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | Meaningful.<br> Broader and more consistent coverage of the advice book.                         |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Cost<br> reduction   | Modest. Monitoring teams are relatively small.                                                   |
| Risk<br> & control   | Meaningful.<br> Stronger Consumer Duty evidence; more consistent second-line oversight.          |
| Capacity<br> release | Meaningful. Monitoring officers spend time on actual<br> reviews rather than sampling mechanics. |

#### Key risks and caveats

Suitability
judgement remains with humans; AI identification of indicators must not be
presented as conclusions; clear separation between draft observations and
regulatory findings.

# A.8 Credit & Lending

Credit
workflows are information-intensive: the real work is synthesising financial
statements, covenant data, market context and relationship history into a
credible view. Agentic automation can do significant first-draft preparation,
freeing credit officers to focus on judgement.

**UC-10**   **Credit File Preparation**

**Audience / sponsor:** *CIB Credit · Private Banking Credit*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 2

#### Description

Prepare credit
files ahead of credit committee — entity summary, financial statements
analysis, covenant history, market context, risk rating supporting data, and
drafted narrative sections. The credit officer then reviews, refines, and takes
the file to committee.

#### Team composition

Research-Report.
1 Supervisor, 3-4 Researchers (Financials, Covenants, Market, Relationship), 1
QA, 1 Reporter. Named Accountable Supervisor: Head of Credit per segment.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | High.<br> Preparation time collapses; more cases can pass through committee per cycle.    |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | Meaningful. Credit analysts redirected from preparation to<br> analysis.                  |
| Risk<br> & control   | Modest.<br> Quality of the underlying analysis still depends on credit officer judgement. |
| Capacity<br> release | High. Credit officers focus on judgement and client<br> engagement.                       |

#### Key risks and caveats

Financial
statement parsing quality; covenant tracking must be auditable; final credit
decision remains human.

**UC-11**   **Covenant Monitoring**

**Audience / sponsor:** *CIB Credit · Loan Operations*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 1

#### Description

Continuously
monitor covenant compliance across the loan book. Ingest financial reporting
from borrowers, compute covenant ratios, detect breaches and near-breaches, and
route alerts to relationship managers and credit officers with supporting
evidence.

#### Team composition

Monitor-Triage-Escalate.
1 Supervisor, 2-3 Monitors, 1 QA. Named Accountable Supervisor: Head of Credit
Administration.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | Meaningful.<br> Breach detection moves from periodic to continuous.              |
| -------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | Modest. The work is currently under-resourced rather than<br> expensive.         |
| Risk<br> & control   | High.<br> Earlier breach detection materially reduces credit risk tail exposure. |
| Capacity<br> release | Meaningful. Credit officers address breaches earlier and<br> more proactively.   |

#### Key risks and caveats

Borrower data
timeliness varies; covenant definitions in facility documents are idiosyncratic
and require careful extraction.

**UC-12**   **Annual Credit Review Drafting**

**Audience / sponsor:** *CIB Credit · Private Banking Credit*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 2

#### Description

Prepare annual
credit reviews — refreshed financials, covenant compliance, rating review,
exposure summary, and drafted recommendation narrative for credit officer
review.

#### Team composition

Research-Report.
1 Supervisor, 3 Researchers, 1 QA, 1 Reporter. Named Accountable Supervisor:
Head of Credit per segment.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | Meaningful.<br> Annual review cycles currently concentrate workload; smoothed by agentic<br> execution. |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | Meaningful. Relief on a large, structured workload.                                                     |
| Risk<br> & control   | Modest.<br> The review judgement remains human.                                                         |
| Capacity<br> release | Meaningful. Credit officers focus on judgement and client<br> conversations.                            |

#### Key risks and caveats

Facility
document interpretation varies; recommendations must be clearly drafts for
human decision.

# A.9 Risk, Compliance & Audit

Second- and
third-line control functions benefit from agentic execution of high-volume,
preparatory and triage tasks. The key design principle here is reinforcement of
human judgement — agents prepare, monitor and escalate; humans investigate,
decide and opine.

**UC-13**   **Regulatory Horizon Scanning**

**Audience / sponsor:** *Group Compliance · Regulatory Affairs · Legal*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 1

#### Description

Continuously
monitor regulatory publications, consultations, supervisory statements and
enforcement actions across in-scope jurisdictions. Summarise, classify, assess
relevance to Investec lines of business, and route to the responsible
compliance officer.

#### Team composition

Monitor-Triage-Escalate.
1 Supervisor, 3-4 Monitors (jurisdiction-sharded), 1 QA. Named Accountable
Supervisor: Head of Regulatory Affairs per jurisdiction.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | Meaningful.<br> More consistent and timely awareness of regulatory change.                                    |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | Modest. Teams are relatively small; the benefit is quality<br> rather than headcount.                         |
| Risk<br> & control   | High.<br> Reduces the risk of missed developments — a classic long-tail failure mode<br> for regulated firms. |
| Capacity<br> release | Meaningful. Compliance officers focus on assessment and<br> response.                                         |

#### Key risks and caveats

False negatives
(missed developments) are the more material risk; source quality varies; human
review of every relevance assessment is required for high-impact items.

**UC-14**   **Control Testing & Evidence Gathering**

**Audience / sponsor:** *Internal Audit · Operational Risk · Compliance Monitoring*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 2

#### Description

Prepare and
partially execute standard control tests — sampling, evidence gathering,
control procedure execution, and drafting of findings and deficiency write-ups
for auditor review. Humans retain all opinion formation.

#### Team composition

Research-Report.
1 Supervisor, 3 Researchers (Sampling, Evidence, Analysis), 2 QA, 1 Reporter.
Named Accountable Supervisor: Head of Internal Audit or delegate.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | High.<br> Audit and control testing cycles shortened.                               |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | Meaningful. Control-testing teams redirected from<br> evidence-gathering mechanics. |
| Risk<br> & control   | High.<br> More consistent and thorough testing; stronger evidence trails.           |
| Capacity<br> release | High. Auditors and risk officers focus on assessment and<br> opinion.               |

#### Key risks and caveats

Independence
considerations — agent access to systems under review must be carefully
governed; findings must always be framed as draft observations pending human
opinion.

**UC-15**   **Surveillance Alert Triage**

**Audience / sponsor:** *Markets Surveillance · Conduct Risk*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 2

#### Description

Triage market
surveillance alerts (suspicious transaction patterns, market-abuse indicators,
communications surveillance). Disposition clear false positives under strict
policy, summarise and escalate potential concerns with supporting context for
human surveillance officers.

#### Team composition

Monitor-Triage-Escalate.
1 Supervisor, 3 Monitors (Transaction, Comms, Conduct), 2 QA. Named Accountable
Supervisor: Head of Surveillance.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | Meaningful.<br> Alert backlog reduction.                                   |
| -------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | Meaningful. Surveillance teams relieved from<br> pattern-search mechanics. |
| Risk<br> & control   | Meaningful.<br> More consistent triage and stronger audit trails.          |
| Capacity<br> release | Meaningful. Surveillance officers focus on genuine<br> concerns.           |

#### Key risks and caveats

Conduct risk is
sensitive territory; human oversight on every escalation; STOR-reporting
decisions remain firmly human.

# A.10 Corporate Functions

Corporate
functions — HR, Procurement, Finance — operate at scale, with a high proportion
of structured, repeatable work. They are often the last to receive automation
investment, but they offer steady, broadly-distributed benefit and are
excellent candidates once the Platform is mature.

**UC-16**   **HR Policy Q&A and Case Triage**

**Audience / sponsor:** *HR Operations · People Services*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 1

#### Description

Answer employee
questions against approved HR policies (leave, benefits, payroll, mobility),
triage and summarise incoming HR cases for HR Business Partners, and draft
standard responses for HR officer review.

#### Team composition

Case-Investigator.
1 Supervisor, 2-3 Case Investigators, 1 QA. Named Accountable Supervisor: Head
of HR Operations.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | Meaningful.<br> Faster employee responses and lower time-to-triage.                            |
| -------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | Modest. HR Ops is not a very large cost centre, though<br> benefits accumulate.                |
| Risk<br> & control   | Modest.<br> Clear separation between policy Q&A and employee relations cases is<br> essential. |
| Capacity<br> release | Meaningful. HR Business Partners focus on cases that need<br> judgement.                       |

#### Key risks and caveats

Sensitive
content (discrimination complaints, whistleblowing, mental health) must be
routed to humans without agent interpretation; data protection over employee
information is critical.

**UC-17**   **Supplier / Vendor Risk Onboarding**

**Audience / sponsor:** *Procurement · Third-Party Risk Management*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 2

#### Description

Perform
third-party risk onboarding: supplier due diligence, sanctions and adverse
media screening, financial viability check, information security and privacy
questionnaire review, and drafted risk assessment for Procurement and TPRM
sign-off.

#### Team composition

Research-Report.
1 Supervisor, 3 Researchers (Due Diligence, Screening, Security), 1 QA, 1
Reporter. Named Accountable Supervisor: Head of Third-Party Risk.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | Meaningful.<br> Vendor onboarding backlogs reduced.                             |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | Meaningful. TPRM is a growing area of investment across<br> financial services. |
| Risk<br> & control   | Meaningful.<br> More thorough and consistent vendor risk coverage.              |
| Capacity<br> release | Meaningful. TPRM and Procurement teams focus on<br> higher-risk engagements.    |

#### Key risks and caveats

Third-party
data sources vary in quality; decisions on onboarding remain with humans;
interaction with DORA and equivalent operational-resilience regimes must be
carefully considered.

**UC-18**   **Finance Close Narrative Drafting**

**Audience / sponsor:** *Group Finance · Business Unit Finance*    ·    **Readiness:** Phase 2

#### Description

Draft narrative
sections of the monthly/quarterly close — variance explanations, KPI
commentary, segment narrative, and management-reporting drafts based on the
closed ledger. Finance teams review, refine and finalise.

#### Team composition

Research-Report.
1 Supervisor, 2-3 Researchers (Variance, KPI, Segment), 1 QA, 1 Reporter. Named
Accountable Supervisor: Head of Financial Reporting.

#### Expected benefits

| Cycle<br> time       | Meaningful.<br> Faster time-to-draft during close.                                        |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cost<br> reduction   | Modest. Finance close teams are not large; the benefit is<br> primarily capacity release. |
| Risk<br> & control   | Modest.<br> All reported numbers and narratives remain under finance sign-off.            |
| Capacity<br> release | Meaningful. Finance teams focus on analysis and review.                                   |

#### Key risks and caveats

No statement
should be published without finance officer sign-off; attribution of any
AI-drafted content must be clear internally; market-reporting content is out of
scope.

# A.11 Prioritisation Guidance

The catalogue
is intentionally broad. Selection for the funded roadmap should be guided by a
combination of factors, not impact alone. The following principles are proposed
to guide prioritisation:

•     Start with use cases that have mature playbooks,
well-defined controls, and clear accountability. This favours Client
Onboarding, Payments and Screening use cases early.

•     Sequence complementary use cases within a business to
maximise the benefit of shared tool adapters, integrations and
change-management investment.

•     Prefer use cases where the first-line business is
actively invested in partnering, over use cases where adoption would be
speculative.

•     Balance efficiency-led cases (cost, cycle) with
control-led cases (risk posture) to build a defensible portfolio story for
regulators, boards and audit.

•     Pilot in one jurisdiction per use case before
expanding, to contain regulatory exposure during the learning curve.

# A.12 What Happens Next

Use cases
selected for the funded roadmap will be qualified through the following process
before committing to build:

•     Business sponsor confirmed and a named Accountable
Supervisor role identified.

•     Baseline process measurement (volumes, cycle time, unit
cost, error rates, control burden) completed.

•     Bottom-up business case signed off by the business
sponsor and the Platform product owner.

•     Preliminary control assessment and jurisdictional
review with Risk, Compliance and Legal.

•     Data Protection Impact Assessment where the use case
processes personal data.

•     Model Risk Management registration for the use case.

•     Preliminary team composition agreed — template set,
archetype, scale, approvals model.

*This Annex will be refreshed as
use cases progress through qualification and as new use cases emerge from
business engagement.*
